figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic of N-GINNs. From the state variables x, the framework learns the bivector network L̃(x), the energy network E(x), the entropy network S (x), and the dissipation-potential network Ξ̃(x, x∗); these outputs are then combined through the matrix-assembly block QS (x), the reversible reparameterization L(x) = QS (x)T L̃(x)QS (x), the orthogonal projection P(x), and the irreversible reparameterization Ξ(x, x∗) = Ξ̃(x, P(x)x∗) − Ξ̃(x, 0) − Dx∗ Ξ̃(x, x∗) ∣∣∣x∗=0 P(x)x∗ to obtain the discovered evolution equations. All boxed components in the reversible dynamics and irreversible dynamics blocks are parameterized, but the corresponding parameter notation is omitted in the figure for visual clarity.
Table 2: Simulation parameters for Example 2, including the dataset size, temporal discretization, and physical parameters of the idealized chemical motor.
Table 3: Simulation parameters for Example 3, including the dataset size, loading protocol, spatial and temporal discretization, and material parameters of the viscoplasticity model.

Table A.4: Neural-network architectures and training hyperparameters used to learn the GENERIC components in the three examples. For each example, we report the hidden-layer width and the number of hidden layers, together with the learning rate and the number of training epochs.

Figure 6: Numerical results for 1D Perzyna viscoplasticity on a representative test trajectory. (a)–(c) Displacement u(x, t), (d)–(f) momentum p(x, t), (g)–(i) viscoplastic strain εvp(x, t), (j)–(l) temperature θ(x, t); within each triplet, the top, middle, and bottom entries show the learned GENERIC prediction, the reference solution, and the pointwise absolute error. The trajectory-wise relative ℓ2 error (RL2E) is reported for each field.

Figure 7: Error statistics for 1D Perzyna viscoplasticity across the full test set. (a)–(d) Mean absolute error fields for (a) displacement u, (b) momentum p, (c) viscoplastic strain εvp, (d) temperature θ, and (e) boxplots of the trajectory-wise relative ℓ2 error (RL2E) for all state variables.

Figure 5: Numerical results for the idealized chemical motor. (a)–(f) Comparison of the learned GENERIC prediction (solid) with the reference trajectory (dashed) for a representative test trajectory: (a) position q, (b) momentum p, (c) internal energy ϵ, (d)–(f) mole numbers n1, n2, n3; the trajectory-wise relative ℓ2 error (RL2E) is reported in each panel. (g) Boxplots of the RL2E for all state variables across the full test dataset. (h) Time evolution of the learned total energy E across the test dataset, showing the median and 95% interval. (i) Minimum entropy production, min(Ṡ ), across the test dataset at each time step.

Figure 4: Schematic of the idealized chemical motor (Example 2). A spring attached to the piston couples mechanical motion (q, p) to a reactive van der Waals gas mixture (n1, n2, n3, ϵ) undergoing X1 + X2 ⇌ X3. The gas chamber volume is denoted by V . The gas pressure generates the reversible force F(x) on the piston, while reaction kinetics are governed by a non-quadratic dissipation potential Ξ.

Figure 3: Numerical results for the harmonic oscillator in a heat bath. (a)–(c) Comparison of the learned GENERIC prediction (solid) with the reference trajectory (dashed) for a representative test trajectory: (a) position q, (b) momentum p, (c) internal energy of the heat bath ϵ; the trajectory-wise relative ℓ2 error (RL2E) is reported in each panel. (d) Boxplots of the RL2E for all state variables across the full test dataset. (e) Time evolution of the learned total energy E across the test dataset, showing the median and 95% interval. (f) Minimum entropy production, min(Ṡ ), across the test dataset at each time step.
